metadata.summary: Define microservice boundaries by bounded context and data ownership; each service owns its tables, exposes one API surface, and maps to one team.

content.title: Microservices Service Boundaries and Structure
content.summary: Microservices architecture structures an application as a collection of loosely coupled, independently deployable services. Each service owns its data and business logic, communicating through well-defined APIs. Boundaries follow bounded contexts — not layers, not convenience, not team politics.

why.p: Microservices architecture structures an application as a collection of loosely coupled, independently deployable services. Each service owns its data and business logic, communicating through well-defined APIs.

Independent scaling, polyglot persistence, and team autonomy are only achievable when service boundaries are clean. A poorly drawn boundary forces cross-service SQL, synchronous coupling chains, and shared deployment pipelines — recreating the monolith's problems with distributed-systems overhead added.

list.item: Large applications requiring independent scaling.
Multiple teams (three or more) working on different features where service boundaries cleanly map to team boundaries (Conway's Law in reverse).
Systems needing technology diversity (polyglot persistence is a real need — transactional Postgres for orders, document store for catalog, Redis for sessions).
Applications requiring high availability and blast-radius isolation (PCI scope, PHI scope, regional data residency).
Organizations practicing continuous deployment where independent deploy cadences matter.
A monolith that has been internally modularised (modular monolith) and the seams are clearly load-bearing.
Long-running async workflows where event-driven decoupling beats request/response chains.
Solo or two-person teams — operational overhead (CI per service, deploy choreography, observability) eats more capacity than it returns.
Pre-product-market-fit — domain boundaries are unstable; you will re-cut them three times and pay full distributed-systems tax each rewrite.
Sub-millisecond synchronous chains (high-frequency trading, real-time bidding) — network hops kill the latency budget.
"Microservices for resume" — a modular monolith with clean module boundaries delivers most benefits without the distributed pain.
Tight transactional boundaries across services — if every command spans three services and two-phase coordination, the boundary is wrong.

rule.statement: Each service MUST have single responsibility — it does one thing well, owns one bounded context.
rule.rationale: Single responsibility keeps the service small enough to rewrite, test independently, and deploy without fear.

rule.statement: Services MUST be loosely coupled — no shared database tables across service boundaries, no shared ORM models, no direct imports from another service's infrastructure layer.
rule.rationale: Shared data creates implicit coupling that defeats the purpose of microservices. Schema changes cascade unpredictably.

rule.statement: Related functionality MUST be grouped with high cohesion — everything a service needs to fulfil its bounded context lives inside it.
rule.rationale: Low cohesion forces choreography for every operation, multiplying latency and failure surface.

rule.statement: Each service MUST own its data — its own schema/database/collection, with no direct reads or writes from external services.
rule.rationale: Decentralized data is the foundation of independent deployability. Shared data is a hidden monolith.

rule.statement: Services MUST be designed for failure — every outbound call assumes the dependency will fail; timeouts, retries, and fallbacks are mandatory, not optional.
rule.rationale: In a distributed system, partial failure is the normal state. Services that do not handle it degrade the entire system.

section.name: service-structure
section.p: Each service follows a layered structure: API (routes + schemas), Domain (entities + services + events), and Infrastructure (database + message bus). This maps directly to hexagonal/clean architecture.

example.title: Per-service folder layout
example.code: services/ ├── user-service/ │ ├── src/ │ │ ├── api/ │ │ │ ├── routes.py │ │ │ └── schemas.py │ │ ├── domain/ │ │ │ ├── entities.py │ │ │ ├── services.py │ │ │ └── events.py │ │ ├── infrastructure/ │ │ │ ├── database.py │ │ │ └── message_bus.py │ │ └── main.py │ ├── tests/ │ ├── Dockerfile │ └── requirements.txt ├── order-service/ │ └── ... (similar structure) ├── product-service/ │ └── ... └── shared/ ├── messaging/ └── observability/
example.note: shared/ contains only cross-cutting concerns (auth middleware, tracing helpers, event envelope). Never domain logic.

section.name: service-implementation
section.p: FastAPI with lifespan context managers is the canonical shape for a Python microservice. Startup initializes DB + message bus; shutdown tears them down gracefully.

example.title: FastAPI service main.py with lifespan
example.code:
# user-service/src/main.py from fastapi import FastAPI from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from api.routes import router from infrastructure.database import init_db, close_db from infrastructure.message_bus import init_message_bus, close_message_bus from infrastructure.health import health_router
@asynccontextmanager async def lifespan(app: FastAPI): # Startup await init_db() await init_message_bus() yield # Shutdown await close_message_bus() await close_db()
app = FastAPI( title="User Service", version="1.0.0", lifespan=lifespan, )
app.include_router(health_router, tags=["health"]) app.include_router(router, prefix="/api/v1", tags=["users"])

example.title: FastAPI route handlers with domain service injection

antipattern.description: Distributed monolith — services call each other synchronously for every operation.
antipattern.reason: All services must be up simultaneously; a single failure cascades. The "microservices" label is cosmetic.

example.title: Distributed monolith: sync chain for every operation
example.code: # BAD - services tightly coupled via synchronous calls class OrderService: def create_order(self, data): user = user_service.get_user(data.user_id) # Sync call product = product_service.get_product(data.product_id) # Sync call inventory = inventory_service.check(data.product_id) # Sync call # All services must be up

example.title: Async events + local data caching
example.code:
# GOOD - async events, local data caching class OrderService: def create_order(self, data): # Use cached/local data user_valid = await self.user_cache.exists(data.user_id) product = await self.product_cache.get(data.product_id)
order = Order.create(data) await self.repository.save(order)
# Publish event for other services await self.event_bus.publish(OrderCreated(order))

antipattern.description: Shared database — multiple services access the same database tables.
antipattern.reason: Schema changes to a shared table break all services simultaneously; deployment independence is lost.

example.title: Shared DB anti-pattern
example.code: # BAD - multiple services access same database # user-service and order-service both query 'users' table

example.title: Per-service data ownership
example.code: # GOOD - each service owns its data # user-service owns 'users' table # order-service has 'customer_info' with denormalized user data

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive microservices design as a five-stage pipeline: (1) a domain agent extracts bounded contexts and proposes service boundaries from the spec; (2) a contract agent generates OpenAPI/AsyncAPI/Protobuf schemas and registers them; (3) a code-gen agent produces the per-service skeleton (API + domain + infra) following the structure above; (4) a resilience agent wires circuit breakers, retries, timeouts, and saga/process-manager skeletons; (5) a review agent runs the anti-pattern checklist (sync chains greater than 2 hops, shared DB, missing trace propagation, command without idempotency key, event without schema version). Persist the service map, contracts, and event catalogue in .aidocs/product_docs/service-map.md so subsequent feature work does not re-derive boundaries.
section.prompt: You are a microservices architect. Given the spec in <spec>: (a) Identify bounded contexts; for each: aggregates owned, language of the domain, team(s) responsible. (b) Propose services; for each: name, ownership, sync API surface (OpenAPI), async events emitted (AsyncAPI subjects), data store. (c) For every cross-service operation, classify as: sync request, async event, or saga. Reject any sync chain > 2 hops; rewrite as saga or precomputed projection.

list.item: Boundaries follow data ownership. A service owns its tables. No cross-service SQL. Replicate via events into a read model if needed.
One service, one team, one repo (or per-service folder in monorepo). Cross-team commits to a service are a smell.
Service-level SLOs published. Each service declares latency/error budgets; downstream callers consume them when sizing timeouts.
Versioning policy up front: additive only, deprecate before remove, support N-1 for 90 days. Pick now, stop arguing per-PR.
Local dev: stub external services using wiremock, localstack, testcontainers. Do not expect engineers to run 12 services to ship a feature.
Human-in-loop on boundary changes. Splitting a service is a one-way door. Block auto-merge on changes that move tables/aggregates between services.

Sync-chain reflex: asked to "let order service get user info," agents call user_service.get_user() synchronously inside the request handler. Constrain to async event + local cache lookup; reject sync chains greater than 2 hops.
Distributed monolith on a single repo: agents copy the same User class into every service module and import across boundaries. Lint the imports.
Hardcoded service URLs: http://order-service:8000 baked in; agents skip env/config injection. Force config-driven addresses.
Auth re-implementation: agents copy JWT-validation code into each new service. Provide one auth-middleware lib and require its import.
Local-dev sprawl: agents add a new service without updating docker-compose.yml or Tilt config. Force "if you add a service, you update local-dev manifest."
